name.text: Midterm 2 - Q3V1: Coupled Circuits + Transformer (E-core, Aiding Dots, Resistive Load)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q3V1: Coupled Circuits + Transformer Design (E-core, Aiding Dots, Resistive Load)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 10 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.9 Problems 9.6–9.24.

Scenario

Two magnetically coupled coils have self-inductances \(L_1 = 50\;\text{mH}\) and \(L_2 = 20\;\text{mH}\), with coupling coefficient \(k = {@k_val@}\).

Both coils are wound in the same sense (clockwise when viewed from the top). Current \(i_1\) enters the top terminal of Coil 1, and \(i_2\) enters the top terminal of Coil 2.

The coils carry DC currents \(i_1 = 3\;\text{A}\) and \(i_2 = 2\;\text{A}\).

Part (a) — Dot Configuration (1 point)

Based on the winding description above, select the correct dot placement:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

(This part will be upgraded to drag-and-drop when diagrams are available.)

Part (b) — Mutual Inductance (2 points)

Calculate the mutual inductance \(M\).

\(M = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{H}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.0456

Part (c) — Stored Energy (2 points)

Calculate the total magnetic energy stored in the coupled system.

Recall: \(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 \pm M i_1 i_2\)

The sign of the mutual term depends on the dot convention and current directions.

\(W = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{J}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (d) — Energy Optimization (1 point)

Select ALL changes that would increase the total stored energy:

[[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Part (e) — Ideal Transformer Comparison (4 points, manually graded)

An engineer proposes modeling these coupled coils as an ideal transformer with turns ratio \(a = \sqrt{L_1/L_2} = {@float(a_val)@}\).

  1. Under what conditions is the ideal transformer approximation valid?
  2. What physical effects are lost in the ideal transformer model?
  3. For the given currents, what is the relationship between \(i_1\) and \(i_2\) under the ideal transformer assumption? Does this match the given values?

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V1 (E-core, Aiding Dots, Resistive Load)

Part (a): The dot configuration is aiding.

Part (b): \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = {@M_num@}\;\text{H}\)

Part (c):

\(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 + M i_1 i_2 = {@W_num@}\;\text{J}\)

The sign is + because the dot configuration is aiding.

Part (d): The correct selections are {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (e):

  • The ideal transformer requires \(k \to 1\) (perfect coupling) and infinite inductances with finite ratio
  • Lost effects: leakage flux, core losses, winding resistance, finite inductance (DC magnetization)
  • Ideal transformer: \(i_1/i_2 = 1/a = {@float(1/a_val)@}\). The given currents \(i_1 = 3\) A and \(i_2 = 2\) A may not satisfy this ratio, showing the coils are not ideal.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The dot marks the terminal where current entering produces flux in the reference direction. Check the winding sense.

truefeedback.text: Wrong sign on the mutual term! Check whether the configuration is aiding or opposing. Aiding: +M, Opposing: -M.
You computed only the self-energy terms and forgot the mutual energy term \(\pm M i_1 i_2\).

falsefeedback.text: Not all correct changes identified. Consider how each parameter affects the energy formula.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q3V2: Coupled Circuits + Transformer (Toroid, Opposing Dots, RL Load)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q3V2: Coupled Circuits + Transformer Design (Toroid, Opposing Dots, RL Load)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 10 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.9 Problems 9.6–9.24.

Scenario

Two magnetically coupled coils have self-inductances \(L_1 = 40\;\text{mH}\) and \(L_2 = 10\;\text{mH}\), with coupling coefficient \(k = {@k_val@}\).

The coils are wound in opposite senses on a toroidal core. Current \(i_1\) enters the dotted terminal of Coil 1, but \(i_2\) enters the undotted terminal of Coil 2.

The coils carry DC currents \(i_1 = 4\;\text{A}\) and \(i_2 = 3\;\text{A}\).

Part (a) — Dot Configuration (1 point)

Based on the winding description above, select the correct dot placement:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

(This part will be upgraded to drag-and-drop when diagrams are available.)

Part (b) — Mutual Inductance (2 points)

Calculate the mutual inductance \(M\).

\(M = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{H}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.0456

Part (c) — Stored Energy (2 points)

Calculate the total magnetic energy stored in the coupled system.

Recall: \(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 \pm M i_1 i_2\)

The sign of the mutual term depends on the dot convention and current directions.

\(W = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{J}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (d) — Energy Optimization (1 point)

Select ALL changes that would increase the total stored energy:

[[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Part (e) — Ideal Transformer Comparison (4 points, manually graded)

An engineer proposes modeling these coupled coils as an ideal transformer with turns ratio \(a = \sqrt{L_1/L_2} = {@float(a_val)@}\).

  1. Under what conditions is the ideal transformer approximation valid?
  2. What physical effects are lost in the ideal transformer model?
  3. For the given currents, what is the relationship between \(i_1\) and \(i_2\) under the ideal transformer assumption? Does this match the given values?

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V2 (Toroid, Opposing Dots, RL Load)

Part (a): The dot configuration is opposing.

Part (b): \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = {@M_num@}\;\text{H}\)

Part (c):

\(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 - M i_1 i_2 = {@W_num@}\;\text{J}\)

The sign is - because the dot configuration is opposing.

Part (d): The correct selections are {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (e):

  • The ideal transformer requires \(k \to 1\) (perfect coupling) and infinite inductances with finite ratio
  • Lost effects: leakage flux, core losses, winding resistance, finite inductance (DC magnetization)
  • Ideal transformer: \(i_1/i_2 = 1/a = {@float(1/a_val)@}\). The given currents \(i_1 = 4\) A and \(i_2 = 3\) A may not satisfy this ratio, showing the coils are not ideal.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The dot marks the terminal where current entering produces flux in the reference direction. Check the winding sense.

truefeedback.text: Wrong sign on the mutual term! Check whether the configuration is aiding or opposing. Aiding: +M, Opposing: -M.
You computed only the self-energy terms and forgot the mutual energy term \(\pm M i_1 i_2\).

falsefeedback.text: Not all correct changes identified. Consider how each parameter affects the energy formula.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q3V3: Coupled Circuits + Transformer (C-core, Aiding Dots, Symmetric)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q3V3: Coupled Circuits + Transformer Design (C-core, Aiding Dots, Symmetric)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 10 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.9 Problems 9.6–9.24.

Scenario

Two magnetically coupled coils have self-inductances \(L_1 = 30\;\text{mH}\) and \(L_2 = 30\;\text{mH}\), with coupling coefficient \(k = {@k_val@}\).

Both coils have the same number of turns and are wound in the same direction on a C-core. Both currents enter their dotted terminals.

The coils carry DC currents \(i_1 = 2\;\text{A}\) and \(i_2 = 2\;\text{A}\).

Part (a) — Dot Configuration (1 point)

Based on the winding description above, select the correct dot placement:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

(This part will be upgraded to drag-and-drop when diagrams are available.)

Part (b) — Mutual Inductance (2 points)

Calculate the mutual inductance \(M\).

\(M = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{H}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.0456

Part (c) — Stored Energy (2 points)

Calculate the total magnetic energy stored in the coupled system.

Recall: \(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 \pm M i_1 i_2\)

The sign of the mutual term depends on the dot convention and current directions.

\(W = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{J}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (d) — Energy Optimization (1 point)

Select ALL changes that would increase the total stored energy:

[[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Part (e) — Ideal Transformer Comparison (4 points, manually graded)

An engineer proposes modeling these coupled coils as an ideal transformer with turns ratio \(a = \sqrt{L_1/L_2} = {@float(a_val)@}\).

  1. Under what conditions is the ideal transformer approximation valid?
  2. What physical effects are lost in the ideal transformer model?
  3. For the given currents, what is the relationship between \(i_1\) and \(i_2\) under the ideal transformer assumption? Does this match the given values?

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V3 (C-core, Aiding Dots, Symmetric)

Part (a): The dot configuration is aiding.

Part (b): \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = {@M_num@}\;\text{H}\)

Part (c):

\(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 + M i_1 i_2 = {@W_num@}\;\text{J}\)

The sign is + because the dot configuration is aiding.

Part (d): The correct selections are {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (e):

  • The ideal transformer requires \(k \to 1\) (perfect coupling) and infinite inductances with finite ratio
  • Lost effects: leakage flux, core losses, winding resistance, finite inductance (DC magnetization)
  • Ideal transformer: \(i_1/i_2 = 1/a = {@float(1/a_val)@}\). The given currents \(i_1 = 2\) A and \(i_2 = 2\) A may not satisfy this ratio, showing the coils are not ideal.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The dot marks the terminal where current entering produces flux in the reference direction. Check the winding sense.

truefeedback.text: Wrong sign on the mutual term! Check whether the configuration is aiding or opposing. Aiding: +M, Opposing: -M.
You computed only the self-energy terms and forgot the mutual energy term \(\pm M i_1 i_2\).

falsefeedback.text: Not all correct changes identified. Consider how each parameter affects the energy formula.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q3V4: Coupled Circuits + Transformer (Rectangular Core, Opposing Dots, Tight Coupling)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q3V4: Coupled Circuits + Transformer Design (Rectangular Core, Opposing Dots, Tight Coupling)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 10 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.9 Problems 9.6–9.24.

Scenario

Two magnetically coupled coils have self-inductances \(L_1 = 60\;\text{mH}\) and \(L_2 = 15\;\text{mH}\), with coupling coefficient \(k = {@k_val@}\).

The coils are wound in opposite directions on a rectangular core. Current \(i_1\) enters the dotted terminal of Coil 1. Current \(i_2\) enters the undotted terminal of Coil 2.

The coils carry DC currents \(i_1 = 2\;\text{A}\) and \(i_2 = 5\;\text{A}\).

Part (a) — Dot Configuration (1 point)

Based on the winding description above, select the correct dot placement:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

(This part will be upgraded to drag-and-drop when diagrams are available.)

Part (b) — Mutual Inductance (2 points)

Calculate the mutual inductance \(M\).

\(M = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{H}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.0456

Part (c) — Stored Energy (2 points)

Calculate the total magnetic energy stored in the coupled system.

Recall: \(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 \pm M i_1 i_2\)

The sign of the mutual term depends on the dot convention and current directions.

\(W = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{J}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (d) — Energy Optimization (1 point)

Select ALL changes that would increase the total stored energy:

[[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Part (e) — Ideal Transformer Comparison (4 points, manually graded)

An engineer proposes modeling these coupled coils as an ideal transformer with turns ratio \(a = \sqrt{L_1/L_2} = {@float(a_val)@}\).

  1. Under what conditions is the ideal transformer approximation valid?
  2. What physical effects are lost in the ideal transformer model?
  3. For the given currents, what is the relationship between \(i_1\) and \(i_2\) under the ideal transformer assumption? Does this match the given values?

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V4 (Rectangular Core, Opposing Dots, Tight Coupling)

Part (a): The dot configuration is opposing.

Part (b): \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = {@M_num@}\;\text{H}\)

Part (c):

\(W = \frac{1}{2}L_1 i_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}L_2 i_2^2 - M i_1 i_2 = {@W_num@}\;\text{J}\)

The sign is - because the dot configuration is opposing.

Part (d): The correct selections are {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (e):

  • The ideal transformer requires \(k \to 1\) (perfect coupling) and infinite inductances with finite ratio
  • Lost effects: leakage flux, core losses, winding resistance, finite inductance (DC magnetization)
  • Ideal transformer: \(i_1/i_2 = 1/a = {@float(1/a_val)@}\). The given currents \(i_1 = 2\) A and \(i_2 = 5\) A may not satisfy this ratio, showing the coils are not ideal.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The dot marks the terminal where current entering produces flux in the reference direction. Check the winding sense.

truefeedback.text: Wrong sign on the mutual term! Check whether the configuration is aiding or opposing. Aiding: +M, Opposing: -M.
You computed only the self-energy terms and forgot the mutual energy term \(\pm M i_1 i_2\).

falsefeedback.text: Not all correct changes identified. Consider how each parameter affects the energy formula.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q3: Upload Handwritten Work (Coupled Circuits)

questiontext.text:
Upload Your Handwritten Work for Q3

Upload handwritten work for Question 3 (Coupled Circuits + Transformer Design).

Include:

  • Part (a): Winding analysis and dot reasoning
  • Part (b): M calculation
  • Part (c): Energy calculation with sign reasoning
  • Part (e): Ideal transformer comparison and analysis

Instructions:

  1. Name and student ID on every page
  2. Dark pen on white paper
  3. Up to 2 files: PDF, JPG, JPEG, PNG

Worth 0 points. Supports grading verification.

Teacher notice: Set “Allow attachments” to 2+ in quiz settings.

graderinfo.text:
Instructor Notes

  • Check dot reasoning from winding description
  • Verify correct sign in energy formula (most common error)
  • For Part (e): look for k→1 condition, mention of leakage flux, finite inductance
